brexit.name: Brexit
brexit.docType: A press article on Brexit, years on
brexit.observeQ: What does this vote reveal about the state of British democracy?
observeNotice: A binary choice — leave or remain — for an issue of vast complexity.
A nation almost evenly split: 52 to 48.
brexit.sentence: In 2016 the UK voted to take back control — and reopened the question of what “the UK” even is.
facts5: June 2016 referendum: 52% Leave / 48% Remain.
Driven by sovereignty and immigration (“take back control”).
Left the EU in Jan 2020; transition ended Dec 2020.
Scotland and Northern Ireland voted Remain.
The Irish border became the hardest problem.
brexit.debate: Was Brexit a democratic reassertion of sovereignty, or a costly act of self-harm?
brexit.paradox: A vote to reassert national sovereignty that strained the UK’s own internal union.
brexit.whyMatter: Brexit reshaped Britain’s economy, its parties and the future of the Union — it sits behind almost any current UK debate.
brexit.learnMore: The slogan “take back control” bundled law, money and borders. Leaving the single market and customs union meant new trade friction and regulatory divergence; the Northern Ireland Protocol, then the Windsor Framework, tried to square an open Irish land border with a UK outside the EU.

connect.why: “Take back control” was, above all, a claim about who makes Britain’s laws — Westminster or Brussels.
Ending EU freedom of movement was a central promise of the Leave campaign.
Scotland voted Remain, which revived the SNP’s case for an independence that would rejoin the EU.
Avoiding a hard land border in Ireland became Brexit’s thorniest problem.
A direct referendum overruled the position of most MPs — a clash of direct vs representative democracy.
New trade barriers and labour shortages followed the exit from the single market.

easy: What did the UK vote for in 2016?
When did the UK formally leave the EU?
What did “take back control” refer to?
inter: Why did a majority vote to leave?
How did Brexit affect Scotland and Northern Ireland?
Why was the Irish border so difficult?
adv: Is Brexit a symptom of democratic vitality or democratic failure?
Did Brexit strengthen or weaken the United Kingdom?
Can national sovereignty mean much in a globalised economy?

problematics.label: A tension
problematics.q: How can a vote meant to restore sovereignty end up threatening the unity of the very state it claimed to empower?

problematics.label: A paradox
problematics.q: Why did “taking back control” leave Britain with less control over its own internal union?

problematics.label: A comparison
problematics.q: What does Brexit reveal about the limits of direct democracy when applied to deeply complex questions?

central.q: Is Brexit a symptom of democratic vitality or democratic failure?
plan: Vitality: a sovereign people overruling the establishment by direct vote.
Failure: a binary answer to a hugely complex question, leaving deep and lasting division.
Resolution: Brexit as a mirror of the tension between direct and representative democracy.
central.hook: A single yes/no question asked a divided nation to define itself — and it did, by the narrowest of margins.

res.i: BBC / Channel 4 explainers on the vote and the Irish border.
The Economist & the FT on regulatory divergence.
The UK in a Changing Europe — the specialist source.

brexit.compare: Compare with other EU members’ Eurosceptic movements, and with referendums as a tool (Scotland, 2014).

monarchy.name: The monarchy
monarchy.docType: A press article on the monarchy today
monarchy.observeQ: What does this ceremony reveal about power in modern Britain?
observeNotice: Immense pageantry around an office with almost no real power.
Tradition and continuity staged for a national, televised audience.
monarchy.sentence: The crown reigns but does not rule — a powerless institution at the symbolic heart of the state.
facts5: A constitutional monarchy: the monarch “reigns but does not rule”.
Head of state — not head of government (that is the PM).
The role is symbolic, ceremonial and unifying.
The monarch stays politically neutral.
Charles III since 2022.
monarchy.debate: Is an unelected, hereditary head of state compatible with a modern democracy?
monarchy.paradox: Powerful in symbolism precisely because it is powerless in fact.
monarchy.whyMatter: The monarchy raises core questions of democracy, tradition, national identity and the Commonwealth — and frames the republic debate across the realms.
monarchy.learnMore: Defenders point to stability, soft power and a neutral figure above party politics; critics see deference, class and an unelected office. The death of Elizabeth II in 2022 revived these debates, including in Australia, Canada and New Zealand.

connect.why: The monarch’s powers are exercised on the advice of an elected government — the heart of constitutional monarchy.
The crown sits atop a hierarchy critics link to deference and inherited privilege.
The monarch is head of state of several other realms now debating republics.
For many, the monarchy embodies a shared, continuous national story.
Royal ceremony is one of Britain’s most recognisable global exports.

easy: What is the role of the monarch?
Who is head of government in the UK?
What does “reigns but does not rule” mean?
inter: Why has the monarchy survived in a modern democracy?
What gives the monarchy its soft power?
Why must the monarch stay politically neutral?
adv: Is the monarchy a democratic anomaly or a democratic asset?
Can a hereditary institution remain legitimate in a democracy?
Does the monarchy unite or divide modern Britain?

problematics.label: A tension
problematics.q: How can an unelected, hereditary office remain legitimate at the heart of a democracy?

problematics.label: A paradox
problematics.q: Why is the monarchy’s very lack of power the secret of its survival?

problematics.label: A comparison
problematics.q: What does Britain’s attachment to its crown reveal about its relationship with tradition and change?

central.q: Is the monarchy a democratic anomaly or a democratic asset?
plan: Anomaly: unelected, hereditary, a symbol of class and deference.
Asset: a neutral head of state, stability and soft power.
Resolution: it survives precisely by being powerless — a symbol, not a sovereign.
central.hook: An unelected crown at the heart of a democracy: contradiction, or clever design?

res.i: Documentaries on the 2022/23 accession and coronation.
The Constitution Unit (UCL) on the Crown’s powers.

monarchy.compare: Compare with Australia, Canada and New Zealand — the other realms and their republic debates.

nhs.name: The NHS
nhs.docType: A press article on the state of the NHS
nhs.observeQ: What does the NHS reveal about British values?
observeNotice: Care given without a bill at the bedside.
An everyday institution that doubles as a national symbol.
nhs.sentence: The NHS is less a health service than a national institution — free at the point of use, and permanently strained.
facts5: Founded in 1948.
Free at the point of use; funded through taxation.
A symbol of fairness and national identity.
Under chronic strain: ageing, costs, waiting lists, strikes.
Politically very risky to reform.
nhs.debate: Can a universal, tax-funded system stay sustainable — and can it be reformed at all?
nhs.paradox: So loved it is both protected from cuts and shielded from reform.
nhs.whyMatter: The NHS crystallises debates on the welfare state, the role of the state, solidarity and public spending — and a direct contrast with the US.
nhs.learnMore: Born of the post-war settlement, the NHS was celebrated even at the 2012 Olympics. Today an ageing population, rising costs and staff strikes test it; debate runs between defending the founding principle and arguing for reform or more private provision.

connect.why: The NHS is the most visible pillar of Britain’s post-war social contract.
Funding the NHS shapes every budget and tax debate.
The NHS depends heavily on workers who came from abroad, from Windrush onwards.
Universal care was meant to guarantee fairness regardless of wealth.
Britons routinely name the NHS among the things they are proudest of.

easy: When and why was it created?
inter: Why is the NHS so central to British identity?
What challenges does it currently face?
Why is reform so politically difficult?
adv: Does the NHS represent solidarity or inefficiency?
Is the NHS a model for other countries?

problematics.label: A tension
problematics.q: How can a system be both Britain’s proudest achievement and one of its most troubled?

problematics.label: A paradox
problematics.q: Why does the public’s love for the NHS make it harder, not easier, to save?

problematics.label: A comparison
problematics.q: What does the NHS reveal about how Britain balances solidarity and cost?

central.q: Is the NHS a sustainable model or a national sacred cow?
plan: Model: equity, low administrative cost, true universalism.
Sacred cow: strain, waiting lists and a reform taboo.
Resolution: its symbolic power both protects and paralyses it.
central.hook: Britain may love the NHS too much to fund it properly — or to reform it.

res.i: BBC documentaries on the NHS at its anniversaries.
The King’s Fund analysis; broadsheet health coverage.
Podcasts on the 1948 founding and Bevan.
The King’s Fund & the Nuffield Trust.

nhs.compare: Compare with the US (private), France & Germany (insurance) and Canada (Medicare).

devolution.name: Devolution & the Union
devolution.docType: A press article on the Union under strain
devolution.observeQ: What does this map reveal about the unity of the United Kingdom?
observeNotice: Four nations, very different in size and weight.
One has no parliament of its own — England.
devolution.sentence: The UK handed power to its nations to hold the Union together — and may have loosened it instead.
facts5: Since 1998: a Scottish Parliament, a Welsh Senedd, an NI Assembly.
Asymmetric — England has no parliament of its own.
Scotland’s 2014 independence vote: 55% No.
Brexit set Scotland and NI against Westminster.
Westminster remains, in theory, sovereign.
devolution.debate: Did devolution save the Union by modernising it, or set it on the road to break-up?
devolution.paradox: Powers devolved to save the Union became a platform for breaking it apart.
devolution.whyMatter: Devolution is central to debates on national identity, federalism, nationalism and the very survival of the UK.
devolution.learnMore: Devolution is asymmetric: Scotland and Wales run health and education while England does not have its own parliament. It eased some nationalist pressure but also gave the nations institutions and confidence — fuelling independence movements, above all in Scotland.

connect.why: Scotland and NI voted Remain, sharpening the case that the Union no longer serves them.
The Good Friday Agreement built power-sharing on the principle of consent.
Devolution gave Scottish, Welsh and Irish identities formal political expression.
The Crown is one of the threads still binding the four nations together.
The SNP turned devolved power into a platform for independence.

easy: Which nations have their own assembly or parliament?
inter: Why has devolution changed British politics?
Why is the system called asymmetric?
How did Brexit affect the Union?
adv: Has devolution strengthened or weakened the unity of the UK?
Is the break-up of the UK now inevitable?
Should England have its own parliament?

problematics.label: A tension
problematics.q: How can sharing power with the nations both hold the Union together and pull it apart?

problematics.label: A paradox
problematics.q: Why did a reform designed to defuse nationalism end up empowering it?

problematics.label: A comparison
problematics.q: What does devolution reveal about how a multinational state manages its differences?

central.q: Has devolution strengthened or weakened the unity of the United Kingdom?
plan: Strengthened: a release valve, modern self-government, peace in NI.
Weakened: institutions that gave independence movements a platform.
Resolution: it preserved the Union by transforming it — perhaps permanently.
central.hook: The UK devolved power to save the Union; the real question is whether it loosened it instead.

res.i: Explainers on the 2014 referendum and the Senedd.
BBC Scotland / The Herald vs London papers.
Podcasts on the Good Friday Agreement at 25.
The Institute for Government on the state of the Union.

facts: By far the most populous nation.
Has no parliament of its own — the heart of the ‘asymmetry’.
nations.debate: Should England have its own parliament?
nations.q: Does English identity get lost inside ‘British’ identity?

facts: Voted Remain in 2016 — fuelling the independence case.
nations.q: Has Brexit made Scottish independence more likely?

facts: Has the Senedd, with growing powers.
nations.debate: How far should Welsh devolution go?
nations.q: Can a minority language reshape a national identity?

facts: Power-sharing Assembly from the 1998 Good Friday Agreement.
The only UK nation with a land border with the EU.
Brexit reignited tensions over that border.
nations.debate: Could there be a united Ireland?
nations.q: Why is the Irish border central to Brexit?
